learning_outcomes.description: Articulate an understanding of an Australian (and, where appropriate, Queensland-specific) theatre canon from British settlement/invasion/colonisation to the present including Indigenous perspectives.
Articulate an understanding of how Australian theatre engages with, reflects and helps shape issues of national identity.
Understand how Australian drama differs from and/or complements cultural expression taking place in other forms, such as film, television and literature.
Develop an understanding of an Australian theatrical canon both through independent learning and through group work, where appropriate.

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
This task assesses your engagement in the tutorial program across the semester.

Length - 150 words per week submitted during tutorials, plus in-class engagement.

Weight - 30%. A mark out of three for each of the 10 weeks (Week 2-12, excluding the Ekka public holiday) where there are one handwritten learning response required (one mark) and two marks for the in-class engagement you demonstrate.

Submit the written responses in tutorials; the rest occurs in class.

A full assignment brief will be made available in Blackboard at the start of semester.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit the written responses at the end of your tutorial each week; the rest occurs in class.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
The research essay is designed to enable you to demonstrate your acquired knowledge and research skills by writing on a specific aspect of Australian Gothic drama in relation to one of the key concepts, movements, texts or figures arising from weeks two to four. This task will assess your ability to:

  • answer a relevant and compelling research question
  • provide an analytic approach to the topic and its relevant issues
  • construct a strong argument
  • provide evidence from the text (where appropriate) and/or secondary material to support this argument.


The referencing style used for Drama assignments is MLA 9th edition.

Detailed instructions for this assignment will be distributed in class and posted on Blackboard at the beginning of semester.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
In a small group (4-6 students), deliver a 10-minute group performance to the class on one of the following plays comprising the late twentieth and early twenty-first century sections of the course: Summer of the Seventeenth Doll, The Season at Sarsaparilla by Patrick White, White With Wire Wheels by Jack Hibberd, Historia by Noelle Janaczewska, Single Asian Female by Michelle Law, Hubris and Humiliation by Lewis Treston.

This is a group performance project. Group members will each receive the same grade out of 30 for the performance.

The performances will take place during your tutorial slot in Week 12 of semester.

A full assignment brief will be provided in Blackboard during the semester.

Group performances will take place in the Avalon Theatre.


Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment

  • All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)
  • Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.
  • Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of:
  • Moderation of marking;
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1 - Introduction to Australian Drama

Overview of the course and a chronological overview of Australian theatre history from the convict era to the present.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1 - Introduction to Australian Drama Overview of the course and a chronological overview of Australian theatre history from the convict era to the present.

learning_activities.topic: Week 1 - No tutorials
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1 - No tutorials

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 - Bush Gothic Drama

The lecture will provide students with an introduction to Australian Gothic drama, defining key terms, before turning to early twentieth-century genre, Bush Gothic and its more recent postcolonial rejoinders. Holy Day by Andrew Bovell is the set text.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2 - Bush Gothic Drama The lecture will provide students with an introduction to Australian Gothic drama, defining key terms, before turning to early twentieth-century genre, Bush Gothic and its more recent postcolonial rejoinders. Holy Day by Andrew Bovell is the set text.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 - Bush Gothic Drama

Tutorials will be devoted to discussing and activating the set performance text (Holy Day) for this week, and engaging with discussion of key terms, lecture content and other set or recommended reading.


Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2 - Bush Gothic Drama Tutorials will be devoted to discussing and activating the set performance text ( Holy Day ) for this week, and engaging with discussion of key terms, lecture content and other set or recommended reading.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 - Contemporary Gothic Drama

This lecture provides an overview of Australian Gothic drama from the late twentieth century through to the present, using the set text (Mortido by Angela Betzien) as the contemporary example.


Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4 - Contemporary Gothic Drama This lecture provides an overview of Australian Gothic drama from the late twentieth century through to the present, using the set text ( Mortido by Angela Betzien) as the contemporary example.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5 - Blak Gothic Drama

The lecture provides an overview of the burgeoning Australian theatre genre of Blak Gothic drama, using the set text (The Drover's Wife by Leah Purcell) as a contemporary example.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6 - Urban Drama

The lecture provides an overview of the rise of Australian drama with an urban setting in the postwar period of the 1950s, using Ray Lawler's Summer of the Seventeenth Doll as the exemplar.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6 - Urban Drama The lecture provides an overview of the rise of Australian drama with an urban setting in the postwar period of the 1950s, using Ray Lawler's Summer of the Seventeenth Doll as the exemplar.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7 - Suburban Drama

The lecture provides an overview of the rise of Australian suburbia in the 1950s and 1960s, which the set text (Patrick White's The Season at Sarsaparilla) satirises. The rise of federal arts funding and the professionalisation of state theatre companies is also discussed.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7 - Suburban Drama The lecture provides an overview of the rise of Australian suburbia in the 1950s and 1960s, which the set text (Patrick White's The Season at Sarsaparilla ) satirises. The rise of federal arts funding and the professionalisation of state theatre companies is also discussed.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8 - The New Wave

The lecture examines the New Wave theatre movement of the 1960s and 1970s. Topics discussed include its urban focus in Sydney and Melbourne (with a brief inclusion of what was happening in Brisbane during this time), and who the movement excluded. Jack Hibberd's White with Wire Wheels is the set text.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Jack Hibberd's White with Wire Wheels is the set text.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9 - Indigenous Drama

This lecture provides an overview of Indigenous drama from its rise in the 1970s through to the present day, examining its changing characteristics over time. Where possible, an Indigenous theatre artist will be invited to provide personal insights and observations. Jack Davis's The Dreamers is the set text.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

No lectures or tutorials during the mid-semester break.
learning_activities.topic_description: MID-SEMESTER BREAK No lectures or tutorials during the mid-semester break.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10 - The 1990s: Queer goes Mainstream

The lecture provides an overview of the internationalisation of Australian theatre during the 1990s and its increasing inclusion of voices that were marginal to the mainstage in previous decades. Noelle Janaczewska's Historia is the set text.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 - Contemporary Australian Drama 1

This week's lecture provides an overview of the 21st century explosion of diversity on stage and the rise of young playwrights-of-colour. Single Asian Female by Michelle Law is the set text.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 - Contemporary Australian Drama 2: Writing back to the canon

Final lecture! We finish the course with a new classic that rewrites and revisions the theatrical and literary canon. Lewis Treston's camp homage to Jane Austen, Hubris and Humiliation, is the set text.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
